name: Templo Mayor
description: The Templo Mayor (Great Temple) was the main temple of the Aztec capital, Tenochtitlan, now Mexico City. Dedicated to both Huitzilopochtli, god of war, and Tlaloc, god of rain and agriculture, it symbolized the dual foundation of the Aztec economy: warfare and agriculture. The temple was the center of religious life and state affairs, a stage for sacrifices, rituals, and coronation ceremonies. Its location at the heart of the city underscored its importance as the axis mundi, the connection point between the earthly and divine realms. The temple's successive expansions over centuries reflected the growing power of the Aztec empire. Human sacrifices were performed at the Templo Mayor, offered to appease and nourish the gods, ensuring the continued prosperity of the Aztec people. Its destruction by the Spanish conquistadors marked the end of the Aztec empire and the beginning of a new era in Mexican history. The ruins, rediscovered in the late 20th century, continue to be a vital source of information about Aztec culture and religious beliefs, offering insights into their complex worldview and societal structure. The temple visually represented the Coatepec (Snake Mountain), the mythological site of Huitzilopochtli's birth and victory over his sister Coyolxauhqui and her brothers, the Centzonhuitznahua. The duality represented by Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc was crucial, symbolizing the need for both war and sustenance for the survival of the empire.
subtitle: Heart of Tenochtitlan, Axis of the World

keyMyths.title: Birth of Huitzilopochtli
keyMyths.description: The Templo Mayor represented Coatepec, where Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed to defeat his sister Coyolxauhqui and her brothers. This myth justified Aztec warfare and the dominance of Huitzilopochtli's cult.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Tlaloc
keyMyths.description: Children were sacrificed to Tlaloc at the Templo Mayor to ensure rainfall and a bountiful harvest. These offerings were believed to maintain the balance of nature and guarantee the survival of the Aztec people.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.description: An ethnographic study of Aztec culture, providing valuable insights into their religious practices and beliefs.

sources.work: The Great Temple of Tenochtitlan: Center and Periphery
